none: Dade County metro rail transit : environmental impact statement.

The Metropolitan Dade County Rail Rapid Transit Project proposes to build a 20.5-mile rail system in Miami, Florida, with the help of federal funding. The route will run from Dadeland to Hialeah, mostly elevated, with 20 stations planned. The project aims to reduce car dependency, improve accessibility, and foster economic growth. However, it will also lead to the demolition of buildings and displacement of residents and businesses. Construction is set to begin in 1979 and finish by 1982, with the system opening in 1983.
Dade County metro rail transit : environmental impact statement.

The Metropolitan Dade County Rail Rapid Transit Project proposes to build a 20.5-mile rail system in Miami, Florida, with the help of federal funding. The route will run from Dadeland to Hialeah, mostly elevated, with 20 stations planned. The project aims to reduce car dependency, improve accessibility, and foster economic growth. However, it will also lead to the demolition of buildings and displacement of residents and businesses. Construction is set to begin in 1979 and finish by 1982, with the system opening in 1983.
Metropolitan Dade County Office of Transportation Administration

The Dade County metro rail transit project was proposed to address the growing traffic congestion in Miami and to promote public transportation as a viable alternative to car travel. While the project aimed to enhance accessibility and stimulate local economic development, it faced public pushback due to concerns over the displacement of residents and businesses along the proposed route. Ultimately, the project was completed, and the rail system opened in 1983, significantly changing the transportation landscape in the area.
United States. Urban Mass Transportation Administration
United States. Urban Mass Transportation Administration

"The rapid transit station at Hialeah Park is expected to have beneficial impacts on the facility. The track will become more accessible to residents and tourists in the Greater Miami area." — Mayor Dale G. Bennett, City of Hialeah
